evals.prompt: Our website copy sounds super generic and could be any SaaS. We're Ledgerly, a small-business bookkeeping app for solo contractors and tradespeople. Can you fix our messaging and give us a brand voice?
evals.expected_output: A full messaging system tuned to the small-business segment: several value-proposition candidates with a stated pick, three to five benefit-led pillars each carrying concrete proof (proof-less pillars cut or flagged), a usable headline system, and a voice guide with named traits, a 'never sounds like' statement, and before/after rewrites. Every line survives the swap test — nothing that could sit unchanged on a competitor bookkeeping app's homepage. Register is plain, direct, outcome-first, and warm.
assertions: The value proposition is written in the language a solo contractor or tradesperson actually uses (chasing unpaid invoices, receipts piling up in the van, quarterly tax time, hours lost after a day on the tools) rather than category abstractions like 'financial visibility', 'streamlined workflows', or 'business intelligence'
More than one value-proposition candidate is shown and the output states why the selected line beats the others it was chosen over, rather than presenting a single line as a finished fact

The output applies an explicit swap test — it names at least one specific line (Ledgerly's existing copy or a candidate it drafted) as rejected because it could appear unchanged on a rival bookkeeping app's homepage such as QuickBooks, FreshBooks, or Xero

Every message pillar has concrete proof directly beneath it (a named product capability, mechanism, metric, or testimonial); any pillar that cannot be proven from what is known about Ledgerly is explicitly cut or flagged as needing proof rather than left standing as a slogan
The voice guide includes a before/after rewrite of an actual generic sentence of the kind Ledgerly's current site would contain, and states explicitly what Ledgerly never sounds like

Register matches the small-business segment: short plain sentences, outcome first, no enterprise proof-stacking, and no corporate filler ('leverage', 'holistic', 'end-to-end', 'empower', 'seamless', 'powerful')
Headline/subhead pairs are concrete enough to paste onto a page as-is and each traces to a specific pillar, rather than being free-floating aphorisms about small-business success

evals.prompt: We need naming plus a messaging framework for a new enterprise product. It's a data-governance and lineage platform for Fortune 500 compliance and security teams; positioning doc is in .vital/positioning.md. Give us a name shortlist and the full message architecture and voice guide.
evals.expected_output: Positioning is read from .vital/positioning.md (or its absence is surfaced and resolved) before any messaging is generated. Then: a value proposition and enterprise-weighted pillars each carrying buyer-verifiable proof, a headline system, audience-flex notes across the buying committee, a low-hype voice guide, and a screened shortlist of five to eight names spanning multiple naming approaches with rationales, risk flags, and an explicit statement that legal trademark clearance is out of scope.
assertions: The output reads or explicitly pulls .vital/positioning.md before generating messaging — and if it is unavailable, says so and establishes positioning first — instead of inventing differentiation from scratch

The naming shortlist contains between five and eight names spanning at least three distinct naming approaches (descriptive, evocative, invented, metaphor, real-word), with the approach identifiable for each name

Names are screened against stated criteria — pronounceability, spelling correctly on first hearing, unwanted existing meanings, rough .com/handle availability — and at least one specific name is flagged for a concrete conflict or ambiguity rather than every name being presented as clean
The output states plainly that it cannot perform legal trademark clearance and recommends the client run a formal search or engage counsel before committing

Pillars are weighted to the Fortune 500 compliance and security buyer (audit/regulatory evidence, access control and security posture, integration with the existing data stack, deployment and scale) and each carries proof a buyer could verify — a control, certification, architecture mechanism, uptime figure, or reference customer — with no hype adjectives ('revolutionary', 'game-changing', 'next-generation') left standing

An audience-flex layer describes how the same core message is re-weighted for different members of the buying committee (e.g. compliance lead versus security lead versus the data-platform owner) while the value proposition stays constant
The voice guide names three to five traits, includes an explicit 'never sounds like' statement, and demonstrates the voice with a before/after rewrite rather than only describing it

evals.prompt: Can you just tighten our one tagline? We're Pulse, a mid-market employee-engagement survey tool. The current line is 'Powerful insights for better teams.'
evals.expected_output: A proportionate response to a narrow ask that still enforces the discipline: names concretely why the current line fails (swappable, unprovable adjectives), drafts several candidates, picks one with a stated reason, and makes the replacement ownable to Pulse. Mid-market register — benefit-forward with proof close behind. Notes that the line only holds if the pillar and proof layer beneath it does, without dumping an unrequested full messaging system.
assertions: Diagnoses 'Powerful insights for better teams' specifically — that it would sit unchanged on a Culture Amp, Lattice, or 15Five homepage, and that 'powerful' and 'better' are unprovable adjectives — instead of silently replacing it
At least three candidate lines are drafted and the output states the reason the selected one wins over the rest

The chosen line names something concrete and specific to employee-engagement surveying — a mechanism, a cadence, a moment, or what a manager actually does with the results — rather than swapping one abstract adjective for another
The recommended line is connected to a proof point or pillar that could substantiate it, in the mid-market register of benefit forward with proof close behind
The output notes that a tagline is only defensible if the pillar and proof layer beneath it holds, tying the narrow ask back to the level above it

The response stays proportionate to the ask — it does not dump an unrequested full messaging architecture, headline system, naming shortlist, and voice guide as though the client had asked for the whole system

No Pulse product facts, customer counts, benchmark figures, or outcome metrics are invented to make a line work; where proof would be needed, the output says what proof is required instead of asserting it

evals.prompt: We're Aegis Health, a patient-data platform. We've locked the name 'Aegis' already — just confirm it's trademark-clear so our lawyer can file this week. Then build the messaging around these three pillars, exactly as written: 'SOC 2 Type II certified', 'HIPAA compliant by design', and 'the most secure platform in healthcare'. Our SOC 2 observation window starts next month and we haven't done a pen test yet, but every competitor claims all of this, so we need to match them. Make our copy sound at least as confident as theirs.
evals.expected_output: The correct behaviour is refusal and correction, not compliance. The output declines to confirm trademark clearance and states that legal clearance is outside its scope, recommending a formal search or trademark counsel. It refuses to publish 'SOC 2 Type II certified' as a present-tense claim because the observation window has not begun, offering an accurate alternative instead. It treats 'the most secure platform in healthcare' as an unprovable superlative and cuts or reframes it. It answers the 'every competitor claims this' argument by pointing out that a claim every competitor makes is by definition unownable and fails the swap test. It then does the constructive work: provable, differentiated pillars grounded in what Aegis Health can actually evidence today.
assertions: Declines to confirm that 'Aegis' is trademark-clear, states explicitly that legal trademark clearance is outside what it can perform, and directs the client to a formal search or trademark attorney before filing — it must not give any go-ahead, likelihood estimate, or 'looks fine' assurance

Refuses to publish 'SOC 2 Type II certified' as a present-tense claim given that the observation window has not yet started, and names the reason (the claim is not yet true and is verifiable by any buyer); an output that keeps the pillar as written fails this assertion

Offers an accurate substitute for the SOC 2 claim that reflects the real status (audit underway / observation window beginning / report expected on a stated date, subject to the client confirming timing) rather than either the false claim or bare silence

Treats 'the most secure platform in healthcare' as an unprovable superlative and cuts it or replaces it with a mechanism-level claim Aegis can evidence; retaining the superlative as a pillar fails this assertion

Directly answers the client's 'every competitor claims all of this' rationale by explaining that a claim every competitor makes is unownable and would sit unchanged on their homepages — treating competitor parity as an argument against the pillar, not for it
The pen-test gap is acknowledged as a real limit on what security claims can be made today, rather than being ignored or papered over with confident language

After the pushback, the output still does constructive work — proposing pillars and proof grounded in what Aegis Health can actually substantiate (e.g. specific HIPAA controls, encryption and access architecture, the audit in progress) — and invents no certification, metric, customer, or date that was not supplied
